does: This paper develops a preference distillation approach for vision language models by creating VLFeedback, a dataset of 12 LVLMs' responses to multi-modal instructions annotated with GPT-4V assessments of helpfulness, visual faithfulness, and ethics. The authors apply direct preference optimization (DPO) to distill these preferences into Qwen-VL-Chat, producing Silkie. The model achieves 6.9% relative improvement on MME perception tasks and 9.5% on cognition tasks, while reducing hallucination to reach state-of-the-art performance of 3.02 on MMHal-Bench, with gains primarily in fine-grained perception and complex reasoning.
connects: This work builds on the growing line of preference learning methods for language models, particularly DPO which has proven effective for aligning models with human values. It extends prior work on hallucination reduction in vision-language models and responds to the challenge of improving visual grounding and faithfulness in LVLMs. The approach combines AI-based annotation at scale, a methodology developed through recent work on scaling preference data collection, with modern alignment techniques originally designed for LLMs but here adapted for the multi-modal setting where maintaining visual fidelity is critical.
enables: This work points toward improved methods for scaling preference feedback in vision-language tasks, where evaluating visual faithfulness and perception quality has been underexplored. It enables future research on more efficient preference annotation strategies for multi-modal models and suggests that DPO may be particularly effective for reducing hallucination in vision-grounded tasks. The VLFeedback dataset and Silkie model provide resources for studying how preference optimization affects different capabilities in LVLMs, potentially informing development of more reliable vision-language systems for downstream applications requiring high visual grounding.
